item.title: Gaslight macOS Malware, Bluekit BITM Phishing, and Chrome Ad Blocker Injection Risks
item.content:encoded:
Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability — Should Read

Researchers found that the Chrome extension Adblock for YouTube, used by more than 10 million people, contains a dormant remote-controlled capability that could enable arbitrary JavaScript injection on websites without an extension update or store review. There is no evidence it has been abused, but its broad permissions and ties to removed malicious ad-blocking extensions make it a significant security risk.

  • Researchers at Island found that the Chrome extension Adblock for YouTube, which has more than 10 million installs and a Featured badge on the Chrome Web Store, contains capabilities to run arbitrary JavaScript code.
  • Island said the extension includes a dormant remote-controlled script injection path that could be activated by a server-side configuration change without an extension update, store review, or visible sign to users.
  • The researchers said there is no evidence that a malicious payload has been distributed through this capability.
  • The extension has ties to other ad-blocking extensions that were removed from the Chrome Web Store for malware, including Adblock for Chrome, Adblock for You, and AdBlock Suite.
  • Island found that the extension runs on every website visited in Chrome and checks only whether the URL contains the string “youtube.com,” which can be bypassed by placing that string anywhere in a URL.

New macOS malware embeds fake errors to confuse AI analysis tools — Should Read

A new macOS Rust malware called Gaslight, attributed to a North Korean-linked actor, embeds fake system errors and prompt-injection strings to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools. The technique appears aimed at making LLM-based analysis agents abort, truncate, or doubt their findings rather than evading traditional sandboxes.

  • A newly discovered macOS malware called Gaslight hides prompt injection strings and fake debugging data inside its executable to confuse AI-assisted malware analysis tools.
  • SentinelOne attributes the malware with high confidence to a North Korean-linked threat actor.
  • Gaslight is a Rust binary with backdoor and information-stealing functionality commonly seen in similar malware.
  • The malware contains a 3.5 KB payload with 38 fake system messages that imitate developer logs, crash reports, debugging output, and program alerts.
  • SentinelOne says the fake messages are designed to make an LLM-assisted analysis pipeline abort, truncate, refuse analysis, or question the validity of its session.

Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft — Should Read

Bluekit phishing-as-a-service has upgraded to browser-in-the-middle attacks, letting victims interact with a real login page through an attacker-controlled browser so session tokens can be stolen even after authentication. The kit also uses stronger anti-analysis checks and live victim monitoring to evade researchers and improve account takeover success.

  • The Bluekit phishing-as-a-service platform has evolved with nearly 70 new hostnames identified over the past week and has added browser-in-the-middle capabilities for improved data theft.
  • Netcraft reports that Bluekit switched from adversary-in-the-middle to a browser-in-the-middle mechanism that uses the open-source JavaScript library rrweb to serialize the page DOM and stream it over a WebSocket connection to the victim.
  • In Bluekit’s browser-in-the-middle attack, the victim interacts with an attacker-controlled browser session that loads the legitimate login page, while the victim’s inputs are forwarded back to the attacker’s browser.
  • Authentication completes in the attacker’s browser, granting the attacker a valid session token and unlimited access to the victim’s account.
  • Bluekit uses anti-analysis features including randomized CSS filters, a frequently changing obfuscated JavaScript bundle, custom CAPTCHA, browser fingerprinting, and WebRTC-based IP mismatch detection.

Another Russian dairy company reportedly disrupted by cyberattack — May Read

A cyberattack disrupted logistics and accounting systems at Russian dairy producer Ufagormolzavod, forcing manual paperwork and slowing shipments while production continued. The incident is part of a broader series of cyber disruptions affecting Russia’s dairy sector.

  • A cyberattack disrupted logistics and accounting operations at Ufagormolzavod, a dairy producer based in Ufa in Russia's republic of Bashkortostan.
  • Ufagormolzavod's chief executive, Ildar Faizullin, said production had not stopped, but document processing and shipments had slowed and workers were processing operations manually.
  • The company did not disclose who was behind the attack, whether any data was compromised, or when its IT systems would be restored.
  • Earlier the same week, attackers compromised the website of Molochnaya Kukhnya in Bashkortostan and posted fabricated claims about power outages and contaminated products.
  • The incident follows other cyber disruptions in Russia's dairy sector, including a June 2025 outage of the electronic veterinary certification system and ransomware attacks on Semyonishna and Kabosh.

PirloTV sports piracy network disrupted as 44 domains seized — May Read

Authorities and anti-piracy groups disrupted the PirloTV sports piracy network by seizing 44 domains that drew over 950 million visits annually, especially in Latin America. The takedown may hamper illegal World Cup streaming, though PirloTV has a history of quickly moving to new domains.

  • The Alliance for Creativity and Entertainment, UEFA, UC3, and Mexican authorities collaborated to shut down 44 domains linked to the PirloTV sports piracy network.
  • ACE said the targeted domains collectively generated more than 950 million visits worldwide each year, including about 230 million visits from Mexico.
  • PirloTV is a network of websites that aggregates and embeds links to unauthorized live sports streams, primarily soccer, rather than streaming content directly.
  • The service primarily targeted viewers in Latin America, with strong audiences in Mexico and Colombia and additional traffic from Spain and the United States.
  • The article says PirloTV can quickly move to new domains, and some domains indexed by public search engines were still providing illegal sports streams at the time of writing.

Cloud Security Incident Response: 7 Best Practices | Mitiga — Optional

Cloud incident response requires different preparation than on-premises IR because evidence often lives in cloud-native logs, identity events, and API activity rather than disk images. Organizations should centralize and retain logs, enforce least privilege, preserve cloud evidence, test cloud-specific runbooks, and use native tools or partners to respond quickly.

  • Cloud security incident response relies on cloud-native telemetry such as control-plane logs, identity events, and API activity because some cloud and SaaS environments do not allow traditional endpoint imaging or endpoint security tools.
  • Organizations should apply least privilege and zero trust by locking down root users with multi-factor authentication, limiting user and resource access, segmenting systems, encrypting communications, and continuously monitoring for threats.
  • Logs should be configured, centralized, retained for a sufficient period such as one year or longer, and protected from accidental or malicious deletion so they remain available during an investigation.
  • Cloud-native tools such as AWS GuardDuty and AWS CloudWatch can provide proactive threat detection and help identify malicious or suspicious activity in cloud environments.
  • Incident response plans should be tested with exercises such as red teams, purple teams, blue teams, and tabletops, and organizations may augment internal capabilities with a cloud incident response partner.

Maven Support Comes to GitHub Checks and OSS Package Search — Optional

StepSecurity has added Maven support to its GitHub Checks and OSS Package Search, letting Java teams block compromised or newly published risky dependencies in pull requests and quickly find affected Maven packages across their environments. The launch extends protections previously available for npm and PyPI to the Java supply chain.

  • StepSecurity has added Maven as a first-class ecosystem in GitHub Checks and OSS Package Search alongside npm and PyPI.
  • The article cites recent Maven-related supply chain risks, including Shai-Hulud-related compromised npm content being rebundled as Maven artifacts and a malicious lookalike of the Jackson JSON library appearing in Maven Central.
  • GitHub Checks now includes Maven Package Compromised Updates to block pull requests that introduce or update Maven dependencies flagged in StepSecurity's compromised-packages database.
  • GitHub Checks now includes Maven Package Cooldown to block pull requests that add recently published Maven versions until they pass a configurable cooldown window.
  • OSS Package Search can now find specific Maven packages and versions across pull requests, default branches, and developer machines, link CI/CD findings to the pull request that introduced the dependency, and export results to CSV.

The Four Elevations of Effective Fraud Prevention — Optional

Effective fraud prevention requires monitoring beyond isolated transactions, combining account behavior, platform-wide patterns, and network intelligence to detect account takeovers, fraud rings, and emerging abuse faster. The sponsored IPQS article argues that layered visibility reduces false positives while improving real-time fraud decisions.

  • Effective fraud prevention programs require monitoring across customer touchpoints such as account creation, checkout, login, and customer service interactions.
  • The article describes four levels of fraud visibility: transaction level, account level, platform level, and network level.
  • At the account level, device intelligence, spending behavior, geolocation, behavioral biometrics, and step-up verification can help identify account takeover activity by comparing current actions with historical trusted behavior.
  • The example fraud case describes a fraudster using customer service to access a bank account, reset access information, order an authorized user card, and move funds through the account.
  • The article says platform-level and network-level visibility can help identify repeated patterns across accounts, devices, regions, IPs, shipping addresses, phone numbers, and other suspicious data points.

item.title: DCloud Scam Infrastructure, curl CVE-2026-8932, Hoppscotch CVE-2026-50160, and Ukrposhta Cyberattack
item.content:encoded:
From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy — Should Read

Infoblox researchers found that the legitimate Chinese DCloud Uni-App framework underpins a massive global scam ecosystem, identifying more than 236,000 scam domains used for fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering, phishing, gambling scams, and wallet drainers. The research suggests many sites are decentralized templates, but shared infrastructure and fingerprints point to centralized operators behind large portions of the activity.

  • Infoblox Threat Intel identified at least 236,493 distinct second-level domains using DCloud Uni-App as scam infrastructure, including fake crypto exchanges, pig-butchering operations, WhatsApp phishing, fake gambling platforms, brand impersonation sites, and crypto wallet drainers.
  • The RainbowEx scam that affected San Pedro, Argentina was built using DCloud Uni-App and was part of a larger template-based ecosystem rather than a bespoke fraud operation.
  • Infoblox found that some DCloud investment scam sites attempted to remove DCloud fingerprints, and these evasive sites were hosted on bulletproof hosting providers at roughly double the rate of vanilla sites.
  • The Lightning Shared Scooter Co. scam in the United States used DCloud-based websites and apps and caused reported losses including more than $370,700 among more than five dozen victims in Salinas, California alone.
  • Yuechi Sharing Technology Ltd. is described as a currently live DCloud-built investment scam that displays genuine Hong Kong incorporation paperwork and a real FinCEN money-services business registration as legitimacy props.

Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country — Should Read

Citizen Lab says Russian authorities used Cellebrite’s UFED phone-extraction tool to break into dissident Andrey Pivovarov’s iPhone in 2021, months after Cellebrite said it had cut off Russia over human rights concerns. The case suggests legacy Cellebrite systems may continue working offline after a customer is supposedly restricted, raising concerns about abuse by authoritarian governments.

  • Citizen Lab reported that Russian authorities used Cellebrite’s UFED phone data extraction technology on dissident Andrey Pivovarov’s iPhone about three months after Cellebrite said it would stop working with Russia over human rights concerns.
  • Citizen Lab said forensic analysis of MobileLockdown records showed USB connections to a device with a Host ID it had previously attributed to Cellebrite while Pivovarov’s phone was in Russian custody.
  • Court records reviewed by Citizen Lab showed that documents pulled from Pivovarov’s phone were used to build a case against him for carrying out activities of an “undesirable” organization.
  • Cellebrite’s chief marketing officer said any use of legacy Cellebrite hardware in Russia after March 2021 was unauthorized and that Russia remains permanently on the company’s restricted-customer list.
  • Citizen Lab and Access Now urged Cellebrite to stop selling to regimes that have abused its technology and to conduct human rights due diligence before and after sales.

Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack — Should Read

Ukraine’s state postal operator Ukrposhta said its mobile app is temporarily disrupted after an overnight cyberattack on its IT systems. A pro-Russian hacktivist group claimed it breached Ukrposhta and stole user data, but those claims have not been independently verified.

  • Ukraine's state-owned postal operator Ukrposhta said its mobile application is experiencing temporary disruptions following an overnight attack on the company's IT systems.
  • Ukrposhta said its specialists are working to restore the mobile app service, but it did not disclose who was behind the attack or whether any data had been compromised.
  • A pro-Russian hacktivist group called the IT Army of Russia claimed responsibility for the attack and alleged it had accessed a company server and exfiltrated a database containing user information and other internal data.
  • Recorded Future News said it could not independently verify the IT Army of Russia's claims.
  • Ukrposhta has about 32,000 employees and more than 6,000 post offices nationwide, and it previously reported a 2024 partner infrastructure incident that disrupted payment processing, customer accounts, APIs, and parcel deliveries.

A Compromised Vendor Account Sent an ACH Redirect With a Fabricated Closure Deadline and Mismatched Phone Numbers — May Read

A compromised legitimate vendor email account was used to request an urgent ACH banking change, bypassing normal authentication-based defenses because SPF and DMARC passed. The key warning sign was a duplicated signature block with mismatched phone numbers, underscoring the need to verify all payment-change requests through known out-of-band contacts.

  • An attacker using a compromised vendor account sent a construction firm's accounts payable contact an ACH banking-change request with a same-day account closure deadline.
  • The sending domain was registered in February 2000, and the message passed SPF and DMARC while being relayed through a legitimate third-party email signature service.
  • The email contained no invoice, purchase order number, outstanding balance reference, or prior context for the requested banking change.
  • The message included two copies of the same vendor signature block, and the phone numbers in the two blocks differed by one digit.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI flagged the message at 68% confidence based on behavioral signals including the accounts payable target, financial-process request, time pressure, and lack of supporting documentation.

Introduction to COM usage by Windows threats — May Read

Windows malware often abuses COM and DCOM to access built-in capabilities like Task Scheduler, WMI, BITS, shell automation, and remote execution while hiding behavior behind GUIDs and indirect vtable calls. Analysts should map ProgIDs, CLSIDs, IIDs, and vtable offsets to real interfaces and methods to understand what a sample is doing.

  • Component Object Model is a fundamental Windows technology used by legitimate applications for object activation, inter-process communication, automation, and language-independent component reuse, and the same qualities make it useful to threat actors.
  • Malware frequently uses COM interfaces for lateral movement, execution, download and exfiltration, persistence, evasion, system discovery, and automation of built-in Windows and Office functionality.
  • Reverse engineering COM-heavy binaries requires analysts to translate opaque GUIDs and indirect vtable calls into meaningful classes, interfaces, and method names.
  • Distributed COM extends COM so a client can activate and use COM objects on another system, with method invocation transported over Microsoft RPC.
  • The article cites Qakbot, Gh0stRAT/SimpleRemoter, Attor, and WarmCookie as examples of malware families using COM interfaces such as WMI, Task Scheduler, and BITS.

Inside the 2026 SMB threat landscape: From phishing and scams to fake AI tools — Optional

Kaspersky’s 2026 SMB threat analysis finds that attackers are increasingly using fake AI tools, communication apps, phishing emails, and dark web access sales to target small and medium-sized businesses. The main takeaway is that SMBs are now major targets and need stronger employee awareness, access controls, backups, and layered security protections.

  • From January to April 2026, Kaspersky solutions detected 33,352 attacks on SMB users in which malware or potentially unwanted applications for PCs were disguised as five popular AI services, almost five times more than in 2025.
  • Popular messengers and communication services remained the most widespread lure, with Kaspersky blocking 414,736 attacks on SMB users involving fake communication apps from January to April 2026.
  • Kaspersky observed phishing campaigns and scams targeting businesses and entrepreneurs that mimicked financial services, AI services, social networks, cloud document notifications, compliance notices, and meeting invitations to steal credentials, personal information, or funds.
  • Kaspersky Digital Footprint Intelligence found that posts concerning small and medium-sized organizations accounted for more than half of analyzed dark web posts offering initial access to corporate infrastructures from January to April 2026.
  • Kaspersky advises SMBs to define and update access rules, regularly back up important data, establish clear guidelines for external services, train employees, and use cybersecurity solutions suited to their size and budget.

Breach At The Beach: The Ultimate Entra ID Training Experience — Optional

Varonis launched Breach at the Beach, a free Entra ID capture-the-flag training experience that teaches defenders how real-world attackers abuse identity features, non-human identities, and noisy logs to exfiltrate data. Players can earn CPE credits and participate online or at Black Hat USA and DEF CON 34 in 2026.

  • Varonis Threat Labs researchers Doron Kapah and Mark Vaitsman created Breach at the Beach as an Entra ID training experience focused on what data exfiltration in Entra ID looks like in real-world situations.
  • Breach at the Beach is a capture-the-flag experience in which players follow Pixel, Varonis’ threat-detecting cat, to trace a threat actor’s steps and identify what sensitive data the attacker is pursuing.
  • The challenges are based on real customer-environment cases encountered by the researchers and teach players how attackers abuse legitimate features, investigate raw Entra logs, and work without AI assistance.
  • The article says the rise of non-human identities such as AI agents, service principals, and automated workflows has expanded the Entra ID attack surface and made monitoring and detection more difficult.
  • Breach at the Beach is free to play online, awards 1 CPE credit and a themed badge for each completed stage, and provides a certificate of completion after all four stages are finished.

ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories — Optional

This ThreatsDay roundup highlights how attackers are still getting big results from basic weaknesses like forgotten credentials, trusted app abuse, fake updates, exposed services, and poorly governed devices such as smart TVs. The key takeaway is to patch quickly, revoke stale access, monitor privileged accounts, and reassess “normal” tools and devices that may be quietly enabling compromise.

  • AISLE discovered six vulnerabilities in curl, including CVE-2026-8932, an issue present since curl version 7.7 from March 2001 and fixed in version 8.21.0.
  • A critical flaw in self-hosted Hoppscotch, CVE-2026-50160, allows an unauthenticated attacker to inject configuration keys such as JWT_SECRET and SESSION_SECRET, leading to full server compromise.
  • Spur Intelligence found that 2,058 of 6,038 reviewed LG webOS and Samsung Tizen smart TV apps contained residential proxy software that can relay third-party traffic through the TV owner’s internet connection with user consent.
  • Zscaler reported that a Payouts King ransomware initial access broker used Microsoft Teams impersonation to deliver the Edgecution malicious Microsoft Edge extension and a Python-based backdoor.
  • Klue said a credential originally provided to a third party in 2022 for a limited pilot was exploited by the Icarus extortionists to steal Salesforce data from multiple corporate customers.

Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop — Optional

BleepingComputer will host a July 8, 2026 webinar with Abnormal AI and Novant Health on why account takeovers are hard to detect and how behavioral AI can speed up investigations and response. The key focus is identifying compromised accounts earlier when attackers use legitimate credentials, trusted services, and normal-looking business activity.

  • BleepingComputer will host a live webinar on July 8, 2026 titled "Stop chasing alerts: Automating email security with behavioral AI."
  • The webinar will be presented by Dan Nickolaisen of Abnormal AI and Eric Danneker of Novant Health.
  • The webinar will examine how attackers gain access to legitimate accounts, why traditional security controls struggle to detect account compromise quickly, and how behavioral AI can help investigations and response.
  • The article says modern account takeover attacks often use trusted identities, legitimate cloud services, and compromised business accounts instead of obviously malicious activity.
  • Attendees will learn approaches for identifying account compromise earlier, reducing manual investigation work, and limiting the impact of account takeover attacks.

Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR — Optional

The article argues that security teams should use network detection and response to move beyond alert-driven triage toward evidence-based investigations and active interdiction of threats. It highlights Richard Bejtlich’s Corelight guide as a practical framework for using network telemetry, threat hunting, and AI-assisted workflows to validate and disrupt attacks before they become breaches.

  • The article says many security operations teams struggle during incident investigations to answer what happened, what evidence they have, and whether they are seeing the full context.
  • Richard Bejtlich’s NDR Essentials guide argues that network detection and response helps practitioners validate findings, understand attacker behavior, and support AI-assisted investigations.
  • The guide emphasizes network interdiction, which means identifying and disrupting malicious activity after initial compromise but before attackers achieve their objectives.
  • Bejtlich says threat hunting should begin with a hypothesis about adversarial techniques rather than with alert follow-up, and analysts should use network logs and sessions to validate or disprove that hypothesis.
  • The article says AI can help network detection and response by optimizing alert frameworks, accelerating incident response through agentic triage, and coordinating outputs from siloed security tools.

item.title: Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke; Leo Platform npm Packages Compromised
item.content:encoded:
Mass npm Supply Chain Attack: 20 Leo Platform Packages Compromised — Must Read

Twenty Leo Platform npm packages were compromised in a coordinated supply-chain attack that used a stealthy binding.gyp install hook to steal CI/CD, cloud, registry, and password-manager secrets from GitHub Actions environments. The campaign appears tied to the earlier Miasma operation and can also propagate by abusing stolen npm tokens to publish more malicious packages.

  • On June 24, 2026, an attacker published malicious versions of 20 npm packages in the Leo Platform ecosystem within a window of less than three seconds.
  • The compromised packages contain a CI/CD attack toolkit that steals secrets from GitHub Actions runners, cloud credential stores, package registries, and password managers, then exfiltrates them using the victim’s own GitHub token.
  • The Leo Platform attack uses the same toolkit and delivery mechanism documented in the earlier Miasma campaign, including a Phantom Gyp binding.gyp install hook, a three-layer obfuscation chain, and Bun runtime evasion.
  • The payload can locate the GitHub Actions Runner.Worker process, read process memory to recover masked secrets, sweep credentials from AWS, GCP, Azure, HashiCorp Vault, Kubernetes, npm, PyPI, RubyGems, JFrog, GitHub PATs, and 1Password, and modify GitHub Actions workflow files.
  • The affected malicious versions include leo-logger@1.0.8, leo-sdk@6.0.19, leo-aws@2.0.4, leo-config@1.1.1, leo-streams@2.0.1, serverless-leo@3.0.14, and 14 other Leo Platform packages listed in the report.

New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns — Must Read

A new stealthy in-memory backdoor called Mistic has been linked to the KongTuke initial access broker and is being delivered through ClickFix-style campaigns alongside ModeloRAT. The activity appears aimed at gaining low-visibility access to organizations that can be sold to ransomware affiliates, including those tied to Qilin.

  • A new stealthy backdoor named Mistic, also tracked as MLTBackdoor, has been deployed in suspected financially motivated attacks against organizations in the insurance, education, IT, and professional services sectors since April 2026.
  • Symantec and Carbon Black linked Mistic to the KongTuke initial access broker and reported that it has been dropped alongside ModeloRAT, a Python remote access trojan previously attributed to the group.
  • Mistic uses DLL side-loading with the trusted Microsoft endpoint security tool MpExtMs.exe, runs payloads directly in memory, and includes a kill switch that can terminate and delete itself.
  • The backdoor can upload, download, move, rename, or delete files, create folders, change its command polling interval, execute code from its command-and-control server in memory, and load Beacon Object Files.
  • Broadcom said the targeting appears opportunistic and noted that ModeloRAT has been observed in attacks that deployed Qilin ransomware.

item.title: Cisco SD-WAN CVE-2026-20245 Zero-Day Exploited; Netskope Warns on Shadow AI Risks
item.content:encoded:
Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access — Must Read

Mandiant says an unknown actor exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN flaw CVE-2026-20245 as a zero-day to escalate from a compromised admin account to root access at a communications service provider. The attackers used stealthy anti-forensic steps, including restoring changed passwords and deleting evidence, highlighting the risk of zero-day exploitation on edge network devices.

  • Mandiant reported that an unknown threat actor exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 as a zero-day at least two months before public disclosure.
  • CVE-2026-20245 allows an authenticated local attacker with netadmin privileges to execute arbitrary commands with elevated privileges by supplying a crafted file to an affected system.
  • The incident targeted an unspecified communications service provider and involved elevating a compromised admin account to full root-level access.
  • Mandiant observed two distinct periods of unauthorized activity, one between late 2025 and January 2026 and another in March 2026, but it is unclear whether the two events are connected.
  • The attacker used anti-forensic techniques including deleting files, reversing configuration changes, restoring a changed admin password, and running a validation script to confirm indicators were gone.

AI Turned The Patch Window Into The Threat Window — Optional

AI is making vulnerability discovery and exploitation far faster than organizations can patch, turning the traditional patch window into an AI-speed threat window. The key takeaway is to stop relying on patching alone and prioritize data-centric controls, zero trust access, and visibility into AI apps and agents to limit what attackers can reach before fixes land.

  • Anthropic’s Project Glasswing reportedly surfaced 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across critical enterprise software in a single month.
  • The article says the median time to remediate a high-severity vulnerability is two weeks, while AI can reduce work such as mapping an environment, chaining attack paths, and developing an exploit from weeks to minutes.
  • The author argues that organizations should not focus only on patching faster, but should control what can be reached while waiting for patches to land.
  • The article states that 98% of organizations report unmanaged AI use, only 12% can detect all shadow AI activity, and 91% cannot reliably stop a risky AI-driven action before it executes.
  • The article cites CVE-2025-32711 as a zero-click prompt injection in Microsoft 365 Copilot that used a crafted email to extract data from OneDrive, SharePoint, and Teams and exfiltrate it through a trusted Microsoft domain.

The Parable of the Bullet Holes: AI Security Edition — Optional

The article argues that AI security teams risk survivorship bias by focusing only on issues visible in sanctioned enterprise LLMs while missing bigger risks from public LLM use, shadow AI, and unmonitored SaaS AI. Organizations should prioritize finding and controlling these unseen AI data exposure paths.

  • The article uses Abraham Wald’s World War II analysis of bomber bullet holes as an analogy for survivorship bias in AI security.
  • Organizations may focus on minor policy violations and sanitized PII found in monitored enterprise LLM systems while concluding that their systems are secure.
  • The article identifies public LLM use by employees and unapproved cloud LLM instances created by development teams as unmonitored AI risks.
  • Sensitive customer PII and proprietary source code can be exposed outside enterprise monitoring systems, leaving major failures invisible to audits.
  • The article argues that AI security efforts should focus on finding unmonitored services, embedded AI in SaaS, shadow AI, and personal public LLM accounts.

item.title: npm and GitHub Actions Supply Chain Attacks, Cisco CVE-2026-20245 Zero-Day Root Exploit, and Gaslight Malware AI Evasion
item.content:encoded:
Miasma Mini Shai-Hulud Hits LeoPlatform npm Packages and GitHub Actions, Expands to the Go Ecosystem — Must Read

A supply chain attack campaign tied to the Miasma family has compromised numerous npm packages and a Go module, using novel install-trigger techniques and targeting developer CI/CD environments, GitHub Actions, and AI coding assistants to steal credentials and propagate.

  • The latest Miasma wave compromised 23 npm packages and a Go module by adding a malicious binding.gyp file that triggers hidden JavaScript execution during installation.
  • The payload steals a broad set of credentials from developer machines and CI/CD environments, including npm/PyPI tokens, GitHub tokens, cloud keys, SSH keys, and Kubernetes configs.
  • Attackers heavily target GitHub Actions workflows to harvest secrets, upload stolen data, and plant malicious repository branches and workflows disguised as Dependabot activity.
  • Persistence hooks are embedded for AI coding assistants and IDEs (Claude, VS Code, Cursor, Gemini), causing the malware to execute when a poisoned repository is opened locally.
  • The campaign marker 'RevokeAndItGoesKaboom' directly ties this wave to earlier GitHub Actions compromises, such as the codfish/semantic-release-action incident, confirming shared attacker tradecraft.

Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Zero-Day CVE-2026-20245 Exploited to Gain Root Access — Must Read

An unknown threat actor exploited the Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 as early as 2025 to gain root access on a communications service provider's device, months before public disclosure.

  • Mandiant discovered that CVE-2026-20245 in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager was exploited as a zero-day at least two months before public disclosure.
  • The initial compromise likely involved earlier zero-day authentication bypass flaws CVE-2026-20127 or CVE-2026-20182 to establish unauthorized peering connections.
  • After regaining access via stolen certificates from a prior breach, the attacker exploited CVE-2026-20245 by uploading a malicious CSV file (evil_tenant.csv) to create a hidden root account named 'troot'.
  • The threat actor used anti-forensic techniques, including deleting files, restoring original configurations, and resetting the admin password to its default to remain undetected.
  • The incident highlights a continuing trend of adversaries targeting edge devices like SD-WAN that lack endpoint detection, allowing persistent visibility into internal network traffic.

Bluekit phishing kit adopts browser-in-the-middle for login theft — Must Read

The Bluekit phishing kit now uses the legitimate rrweb library to perform browser-in-the-middle attacks, relaying victim inputs to an attacker-controlled browser to steal session tokens and bypass multi-factor authentication.

  • Bluekit has shifted from adversary-in-the-middle to browser-in-the-middle (BitM) using the open-source rrweb library to relay victims’ DOM and keystrokes to an attacker-controlled browser.
  • The BitM technique allows attackers to steal valid session tokens and gain unlimited account access even when multi-factor authentication is in place.
  • Bluekit employs multiple anti-analysis measures, including randomized CSS filters, obfuscated JavaScript bundles, custom CAPTCHA, browser fingerprinting, and WebRTC IP mismatch detection.
  • Netcraft reported nearly 70 new Bluekit hostnames identified over a single week, indicating active and rapid deployment of the phishing kit.

New Mistic Backdoor Linked to KongTuke in ClickFix and ModeloRAT Campaigns — Must Read

A new stealthy backdoor named Mistic, linked to the KongTuke initial access broker, has been deployed in opportunistic ClickFix campaigns since April 2026 to establish low-visibility persistence and has ties to Qilin ransomware deployment.

  • A stealthy backdoor named Mistic (MLTBackdoor) has been deployed in attacks since April 2026 targeting multiple sectors including insurance, education, IT, and professional services.
  • Mistic is linked to the initial access broker KongTuke (Woodgnat) and is delivered alongside ModeloRAT through ClickFix campaigns that trick users into running malicious commands.
  • The malware uses DLL side-loading via the trusted Microsoft binary MpExtMs.exe, runs payloads in memory, and can delete itself via a kill switch to avoid leaving forensic traces.
  • The threat actor operates a traffic distribution system on compromised WordPress sites and also uses fake Microsoft Teams messages from an IT Support account to distribute malware.
  • ModeloRAT has been observed in incidents that led to Qilin ransomware deployment, showing the group provides access to ransomware affiliates.

New Gaslight macOS Malware Uses Prompt Injection to Disrupt AI-Assisted Analysis — Should Read

A newly discovered North Korean macOS backdoor, Gaslight, uses prompt injection to trick AI analysts into aborting their review, while exfiltrating browser data and keychain contents via Telegram.

  • Gaslight is a Rust-based macOS backdoor and information stealer attributed with high confidence to a North Korean threat actor.
  • The malware embeds prompt injection payloads that feed fabricated system-failure messages into AI-assisted analysis tools, aiming to make them abort or refuse inspection.
  • It uses a Telegram bot API for command and control, supporting commands such as shell execution, process termination, and file exfiltration, and maintains persistence via a LaunchAgent.
  • An embedded Python stealer collects terminal history, installed applications, running processes, system profile, macOS Keychain, and browser data from Chrome, Brave, Firefox, and Safari, then exfiltrates it as a ZIP archive over Telegram.
  • The implant supplies its Telegram bot token at runtime rather than hardcoding it, and self-redacts that token from its own output to avoid exposure in logs or crash artifacts.

Chrome Ad Blocker with 10M+ Installs Found with Dormant Script Injection Capability — Should Read

A Chrome ad-blocking extension with over 10 million installs contains dormant, server-activated script injection capabilities that could execute arbitrary JavaScript on any website without user notice or a store review, though no malicious payload has been observed yet.

  • Adblock for YouTube, a Chrome extension with over 10 million installs, contains a dormant capability to execute arbitrary JavaScript on any website through a server-side configuration change without requiring an extension update or store review.
  • The extension's check to limit its function to YouTube only looks for the string 'youtube.com' anywhere in a URL, allowing it to be trivially bypassed on any site.
  • No malicious payload has been observed using this dormant script injection path, but three related extensions from the same owner were previously removed from the Chrome Web Store for malware.
  • Separately, Palo Alto Networks Unit 42 identified 18 browser extensions impersonating consumer brands to carry out affiliate marketing fraud.

Russia used Cellebrite phone-hacking tool to crack down on dissident after firm cut off country — Should Read

Despite Cellebrite cutting off Russia in March 2021, authorities used the company's legacy UFED tool to extract data from dissident Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone three months later, underscoring the firm's inability to disable existing hardware and prevent continued abuse by repressive regimes.

  • Russian authorities used Cellebrite's UFED to extract data from dissident Andrey Pivovarov's iPhone12 in June 2021, three months after Cellebrite cut off Russia over human rights concerns.
  • Forensic analysis of MobileLockdown records revealed USB connections to a device with a Host ID previously attributed to Cellebrite, confirming the tool's use.
  • Court records show that documents extracted from Pivovarov's phone using the UFED were used to build a case against him, leading to a four-year prison sentence in July 2022.
  • Cellebrite management stated that any use of legacy hardware in Russia after March 2021 was unauthorized, but Citizen Lab said the technology's architecture made it difficult to meaningfully cut off abusive customers.
  • Citizen Lab and Access Now sent a letter to Cellebrite urging the company to stop selling to regimes with histories of abuse and to conduct human rights due diligence.

Order-tracking app Shop abused to push callback phishing attacks — Should Read

Threat actors are abusing Shopify's popular Shop order-tracking app to inject fake purchase receipts into users' histories, making callback phishing scams appear more legitimate and tricking victims into divulging sensitive information or installing remote access software.

  • Threat actors are inserting fake purchase receipts into the order history of the Shop app, a legitimate order-tracking app from Shopify.
  • The fake receipts impersonate brands like Norton, McAfee, Apple, and PayPal and display phone numbers that lead to scammers posing as support agents.
  • Scammers use these calls to trick victims into revealing account credentials, payment card details, authentication codes, or to install remote access software.
  • The abuse is a callback phishing technique that is more effective than email-based methods because users inherently trust the Shop app.
  • Users are advised not to call the numbers on suspicious receipts and to verify any alleged charges directly with their bank.

From San Pedro to Salinas: How a Chinese Framework “DCloud Uni-App” Powers a Global Scam Economy — Should Read

The Chinese DCloud Uni-App framework serves as the technical backbone for a massive, globally distributed scam economy encompassing over 236,000 domains, allowing centralized operators to rapidly build and deploy fraudulent investment platforms, pig-butchering schemes, and other scams; security teams can use its distinct code signatures to track and block this infrastructure.

  • The DCloud Uni-App framework has been used to build at least 236,493 distinct second-level domain scam sites since 2022, with a steep increase in new registrations after the RainbowEx scam gained international media attention.
  • The same investment scam template family has fueled physical-world fraud operations in the United States, including the Lightning Shared Scooter Co. (LSSC) scooter scam and the currently active Yuechi Sharing Technology Ltd. bicycle scam, which obtained a genuine U.S. Treasury FinCEN MSB registration to appear legitimate.
  • Technical fingerprints, hosting patterns, and coordinated domain registration dips suggest one or more large centralized operators control a significant share of the DCloud-built investment scam infrastructure, with more evasive operators favoring bulletproof providers such as CTG Server.
  • Enterprise exposure is widespread but diffuse: over 985 distinct enterprise customers' devices generated more than five million DNS queries to DCloud-built scam domains, indicating employees are encountering these scams on personal devices and corporate networks.
  • The reusable code fingerprints of the DCloud framework and additional scam-specific markers give defenders a reliable way to detect and block this fraud ecosystem at the DNS layer before users can reach the scam websites.

Inside the 2026 SMB threat landscape: From phishing and scams to fake AI tools — Should Read

In 2026, attackers are exploiting the AI hype by disguising malware as popular AI tools—attacks on SMBs using such lures surged nearly 5x—while SMBs remain the primary target for initial access sales on dark web forums, often as stepping stones to larger enterprises.

  • From January to April 2026, Kaspersky solutions detected 33,352 attacks on SMBs that used malware disguised as five popular AI tools, an almost fivefold increase from the same period in 2025.
  • Kaspersky blocked 414,736 attacks on SMB users where malicious software or potentially unwanted applications were disguised as popular communication apps, showing that fake messenger and video conferencing software remains a widespread threat.
  • More than half of dark web forum posts offering initial access to corporate infrastructures in early 2026 related to small and medium-sized businesses, likely because they are less protected and can serve as trusted third parties for larger organizations.
  • Phishing and scam campaigns in 2026 used fake AI services, fake compliance issues from companies like Apple, and fraudulent online document notifications to steal business credentials, personal data, and money.

Your Employees Logged In Without Issue; Attackers Logged In Too. — Should Read

Attackers are increasingly stealing authenticated session cookies to bypass MFA and silently access enterprise platforms like Workday and Snowflake, and organizations must add cookie monitoring to detect these exposures before they lead to breaches.

  • In January 2026, five malicious Chrome extensions were found stealing session cookies from Workday, NetSuite, and SAP SuccessFactors every 60 seconds and sending them to attacker servers.
  • A stolen session cookie lets an attacker bypass login and MFA entirely by importing it into a browser, gaining immediate authenticated access without triggering alert events.
  • Infostealer malware commonly harvests entire browser cookie stores, and infections on personal or unmanaged devices can capture corporate session tokens without IT visibility.
  • The Snowflake campaign of 2024-2025 used infostealer-harvested credentials and session tokens to compromise over 160 organizations, including Ticketmaster, Santander Bank, and AT&T.
  • MFA, credential monitoring, zero trust access controls, and EDR all fail to stop session cookie theft because they protect the authentication event rather than the session token that follows.

Microsoft quietly extends free Windows 10 ESU support to October 2027 — Should Read

Microsoft has quietly extended the free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates program for consumers by one year, now covering security updates until October 12, 2027, to give users more time to upgrade to Windows 11.

  • Microsoft quietly extended the free Windows 10 Extended Security Updates (ESU) program for consumers by an additional year, now available until October 12, 2027.
  • The extension was made without a formal announcement, appearing only in updates to Microsoft's ESU documentation and as an editor's note on a Windows Experience Blog post.
  • Consumers can receive the free ESU by paying $30, backing up their Windows settings to a Microsoft account, redeeming 1,000 Microsoft reward points, or, in the European Economic Area, by logging in to Windows 10 with a Microsoft account.
  • The consumer ESU program is only for personal devices and is not available for systems joined to Active Directory domains, Microsoft Entra, or managed through Mobile Device Management (though Microsoft Entra-registered devices are eligible).
  • Users already enrolled in the ESU program will automatically remain covered until the new October 2027 end date, and a single ESU license can be used on up to ten devices associated with the same Microsoft account.

Ukraine's state postal operator reports app disruption after cyberattack — Should Read

Ukraine's state postal operator Ukrposhta suffered a mobile app outage following a cyberattack claimed by the pro-Russian hacktivist group IT Army of Russia, which alleges it exfiltrated user data, though this remains unverified.

  • Ukraine’s state postal operator Ukrposhta reported that its mobile app experienced temporary disruption following an overnight cyberattack on its IT systems.
  • The pro-Russian hacktivist group IT Army of Russia claimed responsibility and alleged it had exfiltrated a database of user information, though this claim has not been independently verified.
  • Ukrposhta did not disclose the attacker’s identity or confirm whether any data was compromised, and stated no other services were affected beyond the app.
  • The IT Army of Russia emerged in March 2025 and uses cybercrime forums and Telegram to publish stolen data, recruit insiders, and solicit intelligence on Ukrainian infrastructure.
  • Ukrposhta had previously experienced a cyber incident in 2024 that disrupted payment processing and parcel deliveries through an attack on a partner’s IT infrastructure.

PirloTV sports piracy network disrupted as 44 domains seized — May Read

Authorities seized 44 domains linked to the PirloTV sports piracy network, which generated over 950 million annual visits, in a joint operation by ACE, UEFA, UC3, and Mexican agencies ahead of major soccer events.

  • A coalition including ACE, UEFA, UC3, and Mexican authorities seized 44 domains linked to the PirloTV sports piracy network.
  • The targeted domains collectively generated more than 950 million visits per year, with approximately 230 million of those coming from Mexico.
  • The domain seizures occurred ahead of the UEFA Champions League final on May 30 and during the ongoing FIFA World Cup, potentially affecting illegal streaming access across Latin America.
  • Despite the takedown, PirloTV is known for quickly migrating to new domains, and some domains still indexed at the time of writing continued to offer live sports streams from broadcasters like ESPN and Fox Sports.
  • The action marks the first collaboration between ACE and Mexico's Institute of Industrial Property under a newly signed anti-piracy Memorandum of Understanding.

Another Russian dairy company reportedly disrupted by cyberattack — May Read

A cyberattack on Russian dairy producer Ufagormolzavod disrupted logistics and accounting, forcing manual operations, and adds to a growing series of cyber incidents targeting the country's dairy sector.

  • A cyberattack on dairy producer Ufagormolzavod in Russia's Bashkortostan region disrupted logistics and accounting, forcing manual processing of shipments, though production continued.
  • The company did not disclose the attackers, whether data was compromised, or when IT systems would be restored.
  • Earlier the same week, the website of state-owned dairy provider Molochnaya Kukhnya was compromised and false claims of power outages and bacterial contamination were posted.
  • The incident adds to a series of cyberattacks on Russia's dairy sector, including a past disruption of the veterinary certification system and ransomware attacks on Semyonishna and Kabosh.

A Compromised Vendor Account Sent an ACH Redirect With a Fabricated Closure Deadline and Mismatched Phone Numbers — May Read

An attacker used a compromised 26-year-old vendor account to send an ACH banking-change request with a fabricated same-day deadline; the tell was two signature blocks with phone numbers differing by one digit.

  • An attacker used a compromised vendor email account from a 26-year-old domain to request an ACH banking change from a construction firm's accounts payable contact.
  • The email fabricated a same-day account closure deadline (March 31, 2026) to create urgency, with no invoice or prior billing context.
  • The message contained two signature blocks for the same person with phone numbers differing by one digit, indicating at least one was fabricated.
  • IRONSCALES Adaptive AI detected the message at 68% confidence based on behavioral signals without any malicious link.
  • The primary defense recommended is an out-of-band callback to the vendor using a known number sourced outside the email.

DHS chief says president has met with potential CISA nominee; agency plans to hire 600 — May Read

DHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin said the president has met with a potential CISA director and the agency plans to hire 600 people to rebuild after workforce cuts, while also expressing concern about cybersecurity threats from China.

  • Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin testified that the president has met with a potential CISA director, with Palantir CTO Shyam Sankar considered the likely nominee.
  • Mullin said CISA needs about 600 new hires after workforce cuts and that rebuilding the agency will probably take a year once a director is confirmed.
  • Mullin highlighted daily efforts by China to breach U.S. systems and said CISA will be crucial in defending them, noting that private companies cannot do it alone.
  • Mullin requested new clarity from Congress on the limits of CISA’s authority in cybersecurity.

Introduction to COM usage by Windows threats — May Read

Malware often leverages Windows COM interfaces for persistence, lateral movement, and evasion, requiring analysts to map CLSIDs, IIDs, and vtable calls to understand hidden functionality.

  • Windows COM technology enables communication between programs in different languages and is abused by threat actors for lateral movement, execution, persistence, and evasion.
  • Malware families such as Gh0stRAT, WarmCookie, Attor, and Qakbot leverage COM interfaces to interact with Task Scheduler, BITS, and WMI, avoiding detection by using indirect vtable calls instead of spawning command-line tools.
  • Reverse engineers can recover meaningful class, interface, and method names from opaque GUIDs and indirect calls by using tools like OleView.NET, ComView, and IDA Pro's COM Helper.
  • Analysts can hunt for COM usage by searching for ProgID strings or little-endian byte patterns of CLSIDs and IIDs in binaries, then filter results with context to distinguish malware from legitimate software.
  • DCOM extends COM across the network, allowing remote object activation over RPC for lateral movement, recognized as MITRE ATT&CK technique T1021.003.

Webinar: Why account takeovers remain one of the hardest threats to stop — Optional

Account takeover attacks remain hard to detect because they blend in with normal business activity using legitimate credentials, but behavioral AI can automate detection and response to help security teams limit the damage faster.

  • Account takeover attacks often rely on legitimate credentials, trusted devices, and normal business communications, making them difficult to distinguish from normal user activity.
  • Because attackers can blend into normal business operations, traditional security controls often struggle to detect account compromise quickly.
  • Behavioral AI can automate the detection of unusual behavior patterns, investigation, and remediation workflows, helping security teams respond faster.
  • The webinar will examine how phishing, business email compromise, and account takeover attacks lead to compromised business accounts and present practical techniques for reducing response times.

AI Turned The Patch Window Into The Threat Window — Optional

AI-driven vulnerability discovery is outpacing remediation, so organizations must shift from trying to patch everything to enforcing data-centric security and zero-trust access controls that limit exposure while patches are developed.

  • Anthropic's Project Glasswing surfaced 10,000 high- or critical-severity vulnerabilities across critical enterprise software in a single month, highlighting that vulnerability discovery is far outpacing remediation.
  • The median time to remediate a high-severity vulnerability is two weeks, making it impossible to patch everything as AI-driven disclosures accelerate.
  • Attackers also have access to advanced AI models, shrinking the patch window and turning it into a race that defenders cannot win by patching alone.
  • CVE-2025-32711, a zero-click prompt injection in Microsoft 365 Copilot, demonstrates how AI-enabled attacks can exfiltrate data silently, bypassing traditional perimeter and signature-based defenses.
  • 98% of organizations report unmanaged AI use, and only 12% can detect all shadow AI activity, according to a Netskope report.

Cloud Security Incident Response: 7 Best Practices | Mitiga — Optional

Effective cloud incident response hinges on centralizing cloud-native control-plane and identity logs before they expire, adopting least privilege and zero trust to limit blast radius, and proactively testing response plans that account for shared responsibility boundaries where traditional disk forensics don't apply.

  • Cloud incident response depends on cloud-native telemetry such as control-plane logs, identity events, and API activity because there is often no endpoint to image.
  • Under the shared responsibility model, the cloud provider is responsible for security 'of' the cloud, while the customer is responsible for security 'in' the cloud, including their information and identities.
  • Logs are critical for cloud environments, and organizations should configure, centralize, and securely retain them for a sufficient period—likely one year or longer—to ensure they are available during an investigation.
  • Applying the principle of least privilege and zero trust limits the damage if credentials are compromised by giving users and resources only the access they need and continuously verifying every transaction.
  • Preserving evidence in a cloud incident may require storing EC2 instance metadata, EBS disk snapshots, and various logs, rather than creating traditional forensic disk images.

Rural Hospitals Turn to Threat Intelligence Networks to Strengthen Cybersecurity Defenses — Optional

Resource-constrained rural hospitals can meaningfully improve their cybersecurity by joining collaborative threat intelligence networks and adopting basic cyber hygiene practices, rather than relying solely on in-house defenses they cannot afford.

  • More than 2,000 rural hospitals in the United States are chronically underfunded and understaffed, leading cybersecurity to be deprioritized and exposing them to cyberattacks.
  • Cybersecurity experts and threat intelligence initiatives tend to focus on high-value health-sector targets like major hospital networks and insurance companies, overlooking rural hospitals.
  • Limited cybersecurity resources mean fewer defenses, making rural hospitals attractive targets for ransomware and phishing attacks.
  • Rural hospitals are increasingly leveraging collaborative threat intelligence networks and basic cyber hygiene practices to close security gaps and better protect patient data.

The Parable of the Bullet Holes: AI Security Edition — Optional

Organizations suffer from survivorship bias by only monitoring sanctioned AI systems for minor incidents, while catastrophic data leaks happen unseen in public LLMs and shadow AI that are never inspected.

  • During World War II, statistician Abraham Wald revealed survivorship bias by showing that bullet holes on returning planes only indicated non-critical hit areas, while catastrophic damage to engines and cockpits left no survivors in the data.
  • Companies monitoring only their approved enterprise LLMs for minor policy violations repeat this bias, treating visible 'bullet holes' as all risks while ignoring the fact that fatal breaches occur outside the monitored systems.
  • The most dangerous missing data includes employees inadvertently pasting customer PII into public LLMs and development teams fine-tuning unauthorized AI instances with proprietary source code, neither of which appears in enterprise audit logs.
  • Survivorship bias makes organizations optimize for survival in their sanctioned systems rather than preventing the catastrophic, invisible breaches from shadow AI and unmonitored services.
  • The article calls for security teams to become like Abraham Wald and actively hunt for critical gaps in what is not being monitored, such as personal accounts and embedded AI in SaaS.

Health-ISAC AAMI eXhange Interview – Medical Device Security — Optional

Health-ISAC’s Medical Device Security Analyst Taylor Porter discussed the latest threats facing medical devices during an interview at the AAMI eXchange conference.

  • Health-ISAC's Medical Device Security Analyst Taylor Porter participated in an interview at the AAMI eXchange conference on June 25, 2026.
  • During the interview with AAMI Connect, Porter discussed the latest threats affecting medical device security.
  • AAMI Connect hosts Danielle McGeary and Robert Burroughs were pictured alongside the interview coverage.
  • The article provides links to related resources on topics such as rural hospital cybersecurity, AI adoption in healthcare, and China-nexus threat groups.

ThreatsDay Bulletin: Smart TV Proxyware, 24-Year curl Bug, AI Crime Forums + 13 More Stories — Optional

This week's threats underscore that attackers don't need advanced techniques—forgotten credentials, hidden proxyware in smart TV apps, fake updates, and phishing through collaboration tools are still highly effective for compromise.

  • Over one-third of LG and Samsung smart TV apps reviewed by Spur Intelligence contain hidden proxyware that can relay third-party traffic through the TV owner's internet connection without meaningful owner awareness or consent.
  • AISLE discovered six security flaws in curl, including CVE-2026-8932, the oldest reported curl vulnerability, which has been present in releases since March 2001.
  • A maximum-severity flaw (CVE-2026-50160) in self-hosted Hoppscotch allows an unauthenticated attacker to overwrite the JWT signing key via a single HTTP request, leading to complete server compromise.
  • Klue revealed that a stolen credential from a 2022 pilot was used by the Icarus extortionists to breach Salesforce data from numerous corporate customers, including multiple cybersecurity companies.
  • NCC Group reports that nation-state actors are increasingly adopting cybercriminal tactics and sharing infrastructure with ransomware groups to disguise espionage operations.

Breach At The Beach: The Ultimate Entra ID Training Experience — Optional

Varonis Threat Labs launched Breach at the Beach, a free online CTF that trains defenders to detect real-world Entra ID attack techniques, offering CPE credits and in-person prizes at Black Hat USA and DEF CON.

  • Varonis Threat Labs researchers Doron Kapah and Mark Vaitsman created Breach at the Beach, a free online CTF that lets security practitioners practice detecting real-world Entra ID attack techniques through hands-on challenges.
  • The CTF is designed to teach players how threats abuse legitimate Entra ID features, not misconfigurations, and was intentionally built to prevent AI assistants from solving the challenges so that participants genuinely absorb the lessons.
  • Completing all four stages of the CTF awards participants CPE credits, a certificate of completion, and entry into a drawing for a $2,000 gift card if finished by August 6 during Black Hat USA.
  • The exercise is grounded in actual attacks investigated by Varonis researchers, covering modern concerns like non-human identities and AI-related visibility gaps that many defenders miss in their daily work.
  • The researchers will present and assist players in person at Black Hat USA 2026 and DEF CON 34’s Cloud Village, where top DEF CON participants can also compete for prizes.

The Four Elevations of Effective Fraud Prevention — Optional

Effective fraud prevention requires monitoring at transaction, account, platform, and network levels to detect sophisticated schemes like account takeovers early, using behavioral signals and shared intelligence.

  • Effective fraud prevention requires monitoring every customer touchpoint, from account creation to checkout and customer service interactions.
  • Four elevations are necessary: transaction, account, platform, and network levels, each providing deeper context to detect advanced fraud methods.
  • Account-level monitoring contrasts a fraudster's behavior against historical trusted activity, quickly revealing suspicious actions like changing contact info or ordering additional cards.
  • Platform-level monitoring groups accounts to identify fraud rings and multi-account attacks using shared geolocation, device intelligence, and IP resolution.
  • Network-level partnerships allow organizations to act on known fraud indicators (e.g., phone numbers, devices, addresses) reported by peers, stopping attacks immediately.

Surviving the Mythos Era: Richard Bejtlich on the Case for NDR — Optional

Security teams should adopt network detection and response (NDR) with hypothesis-driven threat hunting to improve interdiction and gather defensible evidence during investigations.

  • Alerts are becoming less useful as vulnerability discovery accelerates, so security teams need validated evidence of active exploit and exposure rather than just more raw telemetry.
  • Resilient security programs should focus on interdiction — identifying and disrupting malicious activity before attackers achieve their objectives.
  • Threat hunting must start with a hypothesis about adversarial techniques, then analysts query network logs and sessions to validate or disprove that theory.
  • AI can assist network detection and response by optimizing alert frameworks, enabling agentic triage, and coordinating tools, but human verification remains a critical control point.
  • A 'zero-baseline' strategy for initial alert rules prevents alert fatigue, and every alert should be treated as the start of an investigation, not a conclusive definition of an event.

item.title: simonecorsi/mawesome GitHub Action Compromise and Google Privacy Controls Rollout
item.content:encoded:
simonecorsi/mawesome GitHub Action has been compromised — Should Read

The simonecorsi/mawesome GitHub Action was compromised after an attacker force-pushed malicious code and repointed version tags, causing workflows using those tags to run attacker-controlled code. Users should treat affected runs after June 24, 2026 as potentially compromised.

  • On June 24, 2026, an attacker compromised the simonecorsi/mawesome GitHub repository.
  • The attacker force-pushed malicious commits and repointed several version tags to the malicious commit.
  • Workflows running against the affected tags after the compromise executed the attacker’s code inside their GitHub Actions runner.
  • The article says the attack method is very similar to the codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Actions compromise reported earlier the same day.
  • The article describes the situation as a developing story.

Google releases new privacy controls for activity history, personalization — May Read

Google is rolling out separate controls for Search and Google Play history and personalization, giving users more granular privacy settings. If Web & App Activity is enabled, the new Search Services History and its Save Media option may be turned on automatically, so users should review the settings.

  • Google is rolling out new privacy controls for Search services and Google Play that separate saved history from personalized recommendations.
  • Search services covered by the new settings include Search, Maps, Shopping, Hotels, Flights, Translate, and News.
  • If Web & App Activity is currently turned on, Google says Search Services History and the Save Media subsetting will also be turned on after the transition.
  • Saved media can include images, files, audio, and video from interactions with Search services, and Google says it can be used to develop and improve services and technologies, including AI models and safety measures.
  • Google says users can turn off the Save Media option, delete individual pieces of media from their history, and adjust or delete their activity settings at any time.

item.title: Cisco CVE-2026-20245 Exploitation, codfish GitHub Action Compromise, and Edgecution Malware
item.content:encoded:
Mandiant reveals how Cisco SD-WAN zero-day attacks gained root access — Must Read

Mandiant says attackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN zero-day CVE-2026-20245 after gaining device access, using a crafted tenant-upload CSV to create a rogue root account and take full control. The attackers also used anti-forensic cleanup and may have relied on rogue peering or previously stolen certificates, so organizations should patch and check SD-WAN devices for unauthorized peering and compromise indicators.

  • Mandiant reported that hackers exploited Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN vulnerability CVE-2026-20245 in zero-day attacks to create rogue root accounts on targeted devices.
  • CVE-2026-20245 is a high-severity command injection flaw in Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Manager, Controller, and Validator that allows authenticated attackers with local access to execute arbitrary commands as root by uploading a crafted file.
  • Mandiant said the attackers used a tenant-upload feature in the SD-WAN command-line interface to upload a malicious CSV file named "evil_tenant.csv" that created a new root-level account named "troot."
  • The attackers changed the default admin account password, extracted configuration information for edge devices, controllers, and SD-WAN templates, and later restored the original password to reduce detection.
  • Mandiant observed anti-forensic activity including restoring modified configuration files, deleting the malicious CSV payload and temporary files, erasing evidence of the rogue root account, and running a validation script to confirm cleanup.

Compromised GitHub action codfish/semantic-release-action steals CI/CD secrets — Must Read

The codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised after attackers force-pushed malicious commits and repointed multiple version tags, causing affected CI workflows to run obfuscated code that could steal release secrets such as GITHUB_TOKEN and NPM_TOKEN. Users should stop using the affected tags, check for the listed malicious commits and payload hash, and rotate any exposed credentials.

  • On Jun 24, 2026, the codfish/semantic-release-action GitHub Action was compromised when an attacker force-pushed two malicious commits and repointed sixteen tags, including v2, v3, v4, and v5.
  • Any workflow referencing the affected tags will pull and run the attacker’s code on its next CI run, and these workflows often contain GITHUB_TOKEN and sometimes NPM_TOKEN credentials with publish access.
  • The malicious commits changed action.yml from a Docker-based action into a composite action that first ran a clean pinned version of the original action and then installed Bun and executed an obfuscated index.js payload.
  • The injected index.js payload was 781,580 bytes, had SHA-256 hash 9f93d77d32833a515bc406c46da477142bb1ac2babeecb6aa42f98669a6db015, and contained the dead drop marker string thebeautifulsnadsoftime.
  • The article links the payload to the Miasma credential stealing toolkit, which uses GitHub’s public commit search API to find marker strings and deliver commands through matching commits.

Malicious Edge extension abuses Native Messaging as bridge to malware — Should Read

A malicious Microsoft Edge extension called Edgecution is being used in ransomware-linked attacks to abuse Chrome Native Messaging, escape the browser sandbox, and launch a Python backdoor on victim systems. The campaign starts with fake Microsoft update pages promoted through Teams impersonation, highlighting the need to tightly monitor browser extensions and native messaging host configurations.

  • A malicious Microsoft Edge extension dubbed Edgecution has been used in a ransomware attack to escape the browser sandbox and deploy a Python-based backdoor.
  • The attack begins with an attacker posing as IT support personnel on Microsoft Teams and directing employees to a fraudulent page under the pretense of installing a spam filter update.
  • Zscaler researchers believe Edgecution is deployed by an initial access broker connected to the Payouts Kings ransomware operation.
  • The malware uses Chrome Native Messaging to let the Edge extension communicate with a local Python-based backdoor that can execute shell commands, run PowerShell, run Python code, write files, enumerate processes, and gather system information.
  • Zscaler recommends that organizations strengthen monitoring of browser extensions and enforce strict controls over native messaging host configurations.

Three ‘cybercrime as a service’ operations undercut by Microsoft, law enforcement — Should Read

Microsoft and international law enforcement disrupted cybercrime-as-a-service infrastructure for SocGholish, Amadey and StealC, seizing hundreds of servers/domains, recovering stolen credentials and identifying €41 million in criminal crypto assets. The operation targeted the malware supply chain used to enable ransomware, fraud and broader intrusions.

  • Europol and Microsoft announced that an international two-week operation dismantled 326 servers and 142 domains used by cybercrime-as-a-service operations.
  • The operation targeted infrastructure used to distribute the SocGholish, Amadey and StealC malware families, which other criminals paid to use for further crimes.
  • Investigators recovered about 27 million stolen login credentials and identified crypto assets of criminal origin valued at €41 million ($47 million).
  • Europol said SocGholish was found on 14,971 infected websites belonging to everyday retailers and is tied to the Russian cybercrime gang Evil Corp.
  • Microsoft said disrupting Amadey and StealC together should have an exponential effect because Amadey is mainly used to break in while StealC steals passwords and other sensitive data.

Trust No One: Automating macOS Privilege Escalation at Scale — Should Read

Researchers describe a macOS privilege-escalation technique that lets a standard user impersonate trusted app components through CDHash cache and NIB payload abuse to call privileged XPC services, potentially disabling EDR and MDM tools without admin rights. Vendors should harden XPC client validation using active code-signing checks rather than relying on cached trust.

  • The article describes a macOS privilege escalation technique that allows standard user accounts to silently disable enterprise security products without administrator credentials, kernel exploits, or triggering security alerts.
  • The technique chains CDHash kernel cache exploitation with NIB payload injection to impersonate trusted application components and invoke privileged XPC methods without authentication.
  • The research was validated against CrowdStrike Falcon Sensor, where full sensor unloading was achieved from a standard user account, and against Kandji MDM Agent, where permanent agent deactivation was achieved via a two-phase XPC chain.
  • CrowdStrike has added detections and preventions for all supported versions of the macOS Falcon sensor, and the Kandji issue has been fixed with CVE-2026-39118 assigned.
  • The author built an open-source automated framework called XPC Hunter to discover exploitable XPC privilege escalation surfaces across installed macOS applications at scale.

DraftKings hacker 'Snoopy' sentenced to 18 months in prison — May Read

Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old hacker known as “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for helping compromise tens of thousands of DraftKings accounts in a 2022 credential-stuffing scheme that stole about $600,000. He was also ordered to pay forfeiture and restitution totaling nearly $1.8 million.

  • Nathan Austad, a 21-year-old from Minnesota who used the alias “Snoopy,” was sentenced to 18 months in prison for his role in hacking DraftKings accounts in the November 2022 cyberattack.
  • Austad pleaded guilty in December 2025 to conspiracy to commit computer intrusion and admitted that he and co-conspirators compromised 60,000 DraftKings user accounts.
  • During the attack, the hackers added payment methods under their control to 1,600 accounts and stole $600,000.
  • The U.S. Department of Justice said Austad operated a shop named after Snoopy where he sold access to stolen accounts, and his cryptocurrency accounts received approximately $465,000 in assets.
  • In addition to prison, Austad received three years of supervised release and was ordered to pay $463,684 in forfeiture and $1,327,061 in restitution.

Why Is an AI Image Company Building a Body Scanner? — May Read

Midjourney plans to roll out a consumer full-body ultrasound scanner in spas without FDA clearance, raising major medical-device security, data volume, and regulatory concerns. Health-ISAC warns that spa users may not receive HIPAA protections and that cybersecurity must be addressed early in development.

  • Midjourney has announced its first hardware product, a full-body ultrasound scanner that it plans to deploy in consumer spas.
  • The article states that Midjourney plans a consumer rollout of the scanner with no FDA clearance yet.
  • Health-ISAC’s Phil Englert raised potential data security concerns, noting that a single full-body ultrasound study can produce roughly 800 terabytes of data.
  • Englert said every ultrasound element that emits and receives sound adds to the attack surface, and a threat model would need to consider whether transducers could be manipulated during a scan.
  • Englert warned that a spa charging customers directly without insurance may fall outside HIPAA, so users should understand whether they are customers or patients and what protections apply.

How to evaluate Cloud Detection & Response (CDR) platforms and vendors — Optional

The article provides a checklist for evaluating Cloud Detection and Response platforms, emphasizing that buyers should prioritize broad visibility across cloud, SaaS, identity, workloads, and AI systems over feature counts. The key test is whether a CDR platform can measurably reduce detection time, response time, and blast radius in real-world cloud attacks.

  • The article says Cloud Detection and Response platforms should be evaluated by real operational needs rather than by feature lists alone.
  • A CDR platform should provide visibility across cloud control planes, SaaS, identity providers, workloads, storage, databases, APIs, networking logs, and configuration changes.
  • The article argues that identity telemetry must be central because attackers in cloud environments can use valid credentials and chain access across SaaS, cloud, and workloads.
  • The checklist says CDR platforms should use AI-driven triage, cross-domain correlation, automatic attack path reconstruction, and machine-speed detection to reduce analyst noise.
  • The article says the final decision should be based on whether a CDR platform measurably reduces time-to-detect, time-to-respond, and blast radius across critical cloud, SaaS, and identity paths.
